texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Delay-Doppler Domain Channel Estimation: What if Sparsity is Unknown?
texts.text: Delay-Doppler Domain Channel Estimation: What if Sparsity is Unknown?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zijian Yang, Yulin Shao, Fen Hou, Shaodan Ma
texts.text: Zijian Yang, Yulin Shao, Fen Hou, Shaodan Ma

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -Sparsity in the delay-Doppler (DD) domain enables efficient channel estimation, but the realization-wise sparsity level is rarely known in advance, and it fluctuates. What if we could estimate the channel without ever knowing how many delays or Dopplers are active? This paper answers that question. We propose a sparsity-agnostic structured estimator that requires no prior knowledge of delay or Doppler sparsity budgets. The key idea is to exploit the Cartesian-product structure of DD support (active delays share a common Doppler set) and to select the support dimensions directly from the data via the Bayesian information criterion. We instantiate the framework on an affine frequency division multiplexing system, where the observation model naturally admits an on-grid DD representation. Numerical results demonstrate that it recovers the exact support with high probability and achieves near-oracle channel reconstruction accuracy, consistently outperforming fixed-budget baselines and sparse Bayesian learning. The approach is waveform-agnostic and offers a practical, adaptive solution for DD-domain channel estimation under unknown and time-varying sparsity.
texts.text: Abstract -Sparsity in the delay-Doppler (DD) domain enables efficient channel estimation, but the realization-wise sparsity level is rarely known in advance, and it fluctuates. What if we could estimate the channel without ever knowing how many delays or Dopplers are active? This paper answers that question. We propose a sparsity-agnostic structured estimator that requires no prior knowledge of delay or Doppler sparsity budgets. The key idea is to exploit the Cartesian-product structure of DD support (active delays share a common Doppler set) and to select the support dimensions directly from the data via the Bayesian information criterion. We instantiate the framework on an affine frequency division multiplexing system, where the observation model naturally admits an on-grid DD representation. Numerical results demonstrate that it recovers the exact support with high probability and achieves near-oracle channel reconstruction accuracy, consistently outperforming fixed-budget baselines and sparse Bayesian learning. The approach is waveform-agnostic and offers a practical, adaptive solution for DD-domain channel estimation under unknown and time-varying sparsity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Index Terms -Delay-Doppler domain, doubly dispersive channel estimation, sparsity, Bayesian information criterion, AFDM.
texts.text: Index Terms -Delay-Doppler domain, doubly dispersive channel estimation, sparsity, Bayesian information criterion, AFDM.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sparsity is a fundamental characteristic of wireless channels, especially in high-mobility and doubly dispersive propagation environments where only a limited number of dominant propagation components carry most of the channel energy [1]-[3]. When such channels are represented in the delayDoppler (DD) domain, their energy often concentrates on a small subset of DD coefficients, making sparse DD-domain modeling a natural foundation for pilot-efficient channel estimation [4]. Under an on-grid approximation, the estimation problem reduces to a sparse linear inverse problem, enabling the application of compressed sensing and sparse recovery techniques [5], [6].
texts.text: Sparsity is a fundamental characteristic of wireless channels, especially in high-mobility and doubly dispersive propagation environments where only a limited number of dominant propagation components carry most of the channel energy [1]-[3]. When such channels are represented in the delayDoppler (DD) domain, their energy often concentrates on a small subset of DD coefficients, making sparse DD-domain modeling a natural foundation for pilot-efficient channel estimation [4]. Under an on-grid approximation, the estimation problem reduces to a sparse linear inverse problem, enabling the application of compressed sensing and sparse recovery techniques [5], [6].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the maturity of sparse recovery theory, a critical practical issue remains: the realization-wise sparsity level of the DD-domain channel is generally unknown and can vary significantly from one channel realization to another. This is not merely a technical nuance: in realistic doubly dispersive channels, the number of active delay taps and the number of active Doppler components fluctuate over time and across different propagation environments. As a consequence, many existing estimators still rely, either explicitly or implicitly, on externally prescribed sparsity budgets, manually tuned thresholds, or fixed model orders [7], [8]. Unfortunately, such quantities are rarely known a priori in practice, and mismatches between the assumed and true sparsity levels lead to either support underestimation (missing real paths) or overestimation (introducing spurious coefficients).
texts.text: Despite the maturity of sparse recovery theory, a critical practical issue remains: the realization-wise sparsity level of the DD-domain channel is generally unknown and can vary significantly from one channel realization to another. This is not merely a technical nuance: in realistic doubly dispersive channels, the number of active delay taps and the number of active Doppler components fluctuate over time and across different propagation environments. As a consequence, many existing estimators still rely, either explicitly or implicitly, on externally prescribed sparsity budgets, manually tuned thresholds, or fixed model orders [7], [8]. Unfortunately, such quantities are rarely known a priori in practice, and mismatches between the assumed and true sparsity levels lead to either support underestimation (missing real paths) or overestimation (introducing spurious coefficients).

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: Z. Yang, F. Hou, and S. Ma are with the State Key Laboratory of Internet of Things for Smart City, University of Macau, Macau, China (e-mails: { yc37408,shaodanma,fenhou } @um.edu.mo).
texts.text: Z. Yang, F. Hou, and S. Ma are with the State Key Laboratory of Internet of Things for Smart City, University of Macau, Macau, China (e-mails: { yc37408,shaodanma,fenhou } @um.edu.mo).

texts.text: Y. Shao is with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China (e-mail: ylshao@hku.hk).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A parallel difficulty is that generic sparse recovery algorithms treat the DD-domain channel as an unstructured sparse vector, overlooking the inherent regularity of the DD support pattern [6]. In practice, however, the support is not arbitrary: active delay taps often share a common set of Doppler frequencies, leading to a Cartesian-product structure [9], [10]. Such regularity, when properly exploited, can significantly reduce the ambiguity of support search and improve model selection. Therefore, an important open question is: Can we design a DD-domain channel estimator that (i) requires no prior knowledge of sparsity levels, (ii) exploits the structured nature of DD support, and (iii) adapts to each channel realization in a principled manner?
texts.text: A parallel difficulty is that generic sparse recovery algorithms treat the DD-domain channel as an unstructured sparse vector, overlooking the inherent regularity of the DD support pattern [6]. In practice, however, the support is not arbitrary: active delay taps often share a common set of Doppler frequencies, leading to a Cartesian-product structure [9], [10]. Such regularity, when properly exploited, can significantly reduce the ambiguity of support search and improve model selection. Therefore, an important open question is: Can we design a DD-domain channel estimator that (i) requires no prior knowledge of sparsity levels, (ii) exploits the structured nature of DD support, and (iii) adapts to each channel realization in a principled manner?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we answer this question affirmatively by developing a sparsity-agnostic structured channel estimator for the DD domain. Instead of assuming known delay and Doppler sparsity levels, we infer the effective support dimensions directly from the received observations. Rather than applying a generic sparse recovery routine over an unrestricted support space, we exploit the Cartesian-product regularity of the DD support and construct a structure-aware search strategy. The final model is selected via the Bayesian information criterion (BIC) [11], which balances data fitting accuracy and model complexity without requiring any external sparsity budget.
texts.text: In this paper, we answer this question affirmatively by developing a sparsity-agnostic structured channel estimator for the DD domain. Instead of assuming known delay and Doppler sparsity levels, we infer the effective support dimensions directly from the received observations. Rather than applying a generic sparse recovery routine over an unrestricted support space, we exploit the Cartesian-product regularity of the DD support and construct a structure-aware search strategy. The final model is selected via the Bayesian information criterion (BIC) [11], which balances data fitting accuracy and model complexity without requiring any external sparsity budget.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proposed method is deliberately decoupled from any specific waveform. It requires only a linear observation model relating the received observations to the vectorized DD-domain channel via a known sensing matrix, under additive noise. Such a model arises naturally in various multicarrier systems, including but not limited to AFDM, OTFS, and OFDM with appropriate preprocessing. In this paper, we instantiate the framework using AFDM [12], [13], because AFDM provides a particularly clean DD-domain input-output relation, but the core methodology is transferable to any system that admits an on-grid DD observation model.
texts.text: The proposed method is deliberately decoupled from any specific waveform. It requires only a linear observation model relating the received observations to the vectorized DD-domain channel via a known sensing matrix, under additive noise. Such a model arises naturally in various multicarrier systems, including but not limited to AFDM, OTFS, and OFDM with appropriate preprocessing. In this paper, we instantiate the framework using AFDM [12], [13], because AFDM provides a particularly clean DD-domain input-output relation, but the core methodology is transferable to any system that admits an on-grid DD observation model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our main contributions are summarized as follows.
texts.text: Our main contributions are summarized as follows.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We propose a sparsity-agnostic DD-domain channel estimator that does not require the delay and Doppler sparsity levels to be known a priori. This makes the method practical for realistic channels where the realization-wise sparsity fluctuates from block to block.
texts.text: We propose a sparsity-agnostic DD-domain channel estimator that does not require the delay and Doppler sparsity levels to be known a priori. This makes the method practical for realistic channels where the realization-wise sparsity fluctuates from block to block.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · By exploiting the Cartesian-product regularity of DDdomain support, we develop a structure-aware support
texts.text: By exploiting the Cartesian-product regularity of DDdomain support, we develop a structure-aware support

texts.label: text
texts.orig: search strategy that significantly reduces the search space compared to unstructured sparse recovery, while remaining robust to unknown model order.
texts.text: search strategy that significantly reduces the search space compared to unstructured sparse recovery, while remaining robust to unknown model order.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We establish a BIC-based model selection mechanism that automatically determines the support dimensions and the final support candidate from the data. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high exact support recovery probability and near-oracle channel reconstruction accuracy, outperforming fixed-budget baselines and sparse Bayesian learning.
texts.text: We establish a BIC-based model selection mechanism that automatically determines the support dimensions and the final support candidate from the data. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high exact support recovery probability and near-oracle channel reconstruction accuracy, outperforming fixed-budget baselines and sparse Bayesian learning.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: II. SYSTEM MODEL
texts.text: II. SYSTEM MODEL

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Doubly Selective Channel Estimation
texts.text: A. Doubly Selective Channel Estimation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We first describe the discrete-time doubly selective channel model and its on-grid DD representation. Let s n and r n denote the transmitted and received discrete-time signals, respectively. The doubly selective channel is modeled as
texts.text: We first describe the discrete-time doubly selective channel model and its on-grid DD representation. Let s n and r n denote the transmitted and received discrete-time signals, respectively. The doubly selective channel is modeled as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: r n = L -1 ∑ l =0 s n -l h l,n + z n , n ∈ Z , (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where L -1 is the maximum channel delay, h l,n is the timevarying complex gain associated with the ℓ -th delay tap at time index n [14], and z n ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 w ) is additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). The goal of channel estimation is to recover { h l,n } from the received signal.
texts.text: where L -1 is the maximum channel delay, h l,n is the timevarying complex gain associated with the ℓ -th delay tap at time index n [14], and z n ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 w ) is additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). The goal of channel estimation is to recover { h l,n } from the received signal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To make the problem tractable, we adopt an on-grid DD representation. Each delay tap is expanded over a finite Doppler grid:
texts.text: To make the problem tractable, we adopt an on-grid DD representation. Each delay tap is expanded over a finite Doppler grid:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: h l,n = Q ∑ q = -Q α l,q e j 2 π N nq , l = 0 , . . . , L -1 , (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where q is the Doppler-bin index, Q is the maximum normalized Doppler index, and α l,q denotes the effective DD coefficient at location ( l, q ) . The coefficient is further modeled as α l,q = I l,q g l,q , with I l,q ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicating activity and g l,q ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 α ) the complex gain.
texts.text: where q is the Doppler-bin index, Q is the maximum normalized Doppler index, and α l,q denotes the effective DD coefficient at location ( l, q ) . The coefficient is further modeled as α l,q = I l,q g l,q , with I l,q ∈ { 0 , 1 } indicating activity and g l,q ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 α ) the complex gain.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For convenience, we define B ≜ 2 Q + 1 such that each delay tap corresponds to a Doppler block of size B . The ℓ -th block coefficient vector is defined as α l ≜ [ α l, -Q , α l, -Q +1 , . . . , α l,Q ] ⊤ ∈ C B . Stacking all delay blocks yields α ≜ [ α ⊤ 0 , . . . , α ⊤ L -1 ] ⊤ ∈ C LB . The average channel power is normalized as ∑ L -1 l =0 ∑ Q q = -Q E [ | α l,q | 2 ] = 1 .
texts.text: For convenience, we define B ≜ 2 Q + 1 such that each delay tap corresponds to a Doppler block of size B . The ℓ -th block coefficient vector is defined as α l ≜ [ α l, -Q , α l, -Q +1 , . . . , α l,Q ] ⊤ ∈ C B . Stacking all delay blocks yields α ≜ [ α ⊤ 0 , . . . , α ⊤ L -1 ] ⊤ ∈ C LB . The average channel power is normalized as ∑ L -1 l =0 ∑ Q q = -Q E [ | α l,q | 2 ] = 1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this on-grid DD domain, the channel is often sparse: only a small number of DD coefficients are active. Classical compressed sensing (CS) based channel estimators treat α as an unstructured sparse vector. They typically require either
texts.text: In this on-grid DD domain, the channel is often sparse: only a small number of DD coefficients are active. Classical compressed sensing (CS) based channel estimators treat α as an unstructured sparse vector. They typically require either

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · a priori knowledge of the sparsity level (e.g., the number of active paths), or
texts.text: a priori knowledge of the sparsity level (e.g., the number of active paths), or

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · a fixed sparsity budget or threshold that remains constant across channel realizations.
texts.text: a fixed sparsity budget or threshold that remains constant across channel realizations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, in realistic doubly dispersive channels, the number of active delay taps and active Doppler components randomly varies from one realization to another. Consequently, a fixed sparsity budget inevitably leads to underestimation or overestimation. Moreover, conventional methods ignore the inherent structure of the DD support, which can be exploited to improve estimation reliability.
texts.text: However, in realistic doubly dispersive channels, the number of active delay taps and active Doppler components randomly varies from one realization to another. Consequently, a fixed sparsity budget inevitably leads to underestimation or overestimation. Moreover, conventional methods ignore the inherent structure of the DD support, which can be exploited to improve estimation reliability.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Structured Sparsity in the DD Domain
texts.text: B. Structured Sparsity in the DD Domain

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A general doubly sparse DD support can be factorized as I l,q = I l I ( l ) q , where I l indicates whether the ℓ -th delay tap is active, and I ( l ) q indicates which Doppler bins are active within that delay tap. In many physically relevant scenarios, for instance, when the relative velocity between transmitter and receiver dominates the Doppler effect and the scattering environment exhibits a similar Doppler spread across different delays, the active Doppler bins are common to all active delay taps [10].
texts.text: A general doubly sparse DD support can be factorized as I l,q = I l I ( l ) q , where I l indicates whether the ℓ -th delay tap is active, and I ( l ) q indicates which Doppler bins are active within that delay tap. In many physically relevant scenarios, for instance, when the relative velocity between transmitter and receiver dominates the Doppler effect and the scattering environment exhibits a similar Doppler spread across different delays, the active Doppler bins are common to all active delay taps [10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concretely, there exists a common indicator S q such that I ( l ) q = S q for all l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , and therefore I l,q = I l S q . The support variables are modeled as independent Bernoulli random variables: I l ∼ Bernoulli( p d ) and S q ∼ Bernoulli( p D ) , with mutual independence across ℓ and across q . Moreover, the active complex gains are assumed independent of the support variables and satisfy g l,q ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 α ) .
texts.text: Concretely, there exists a common indicator S q such that I ( l ) q = S q for all l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , and therefore I l,q = I l S q . The support variables are modeled as independent Bernoulli random variables: I l ∼ Bernoulli( p d ) and S q ∼ Bernoulli( p D ) , with mutual independence across ℓ and across q . Moreover, the active complex gains are assumed independent of the support variables and satisfy g l,q ∼ CN (0 , σ 2 α ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Define the active delay set D ⋆ ≜ { l ∈ { 0 , ..., L -1 } : I l = 1 } and the active Doppler set Q ⋆ ≜ { q ∈ {-Q,..., Q } : S q = 1 } . Then the true DD support has the Cartesian product form
texts.text: Define the active delay set D ⋆ ≜ { l ∈ { 0 , ..., L -1 } : I l = 1 } and the active Doppler set Q ⋆ ≜ { q ∈ {-Q,..., Q } : S q = 1 } . Then the true DD support has the Cartesian product form

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The cardinalities are K d ≜ |D ⋆ | = ∑ L -1 l =0 I l and K D ≜ |Q ⋆ | = ∑ Q q = -Q S q . Their means are s d ≜ E [ K d ] = p d L and s D ≜ E [ K D ] = p D B , but the actual K d and K D vary per realization.
texts.text: The cardinalities are K d ≜ |D ⋆ | = ∑ L -1 l =0 I l and K D ≜ |Q ⋆ | = ∑ Q q = -Q S q . Their means are s d ≜ E [ K d ] = p d L and s D ≜ E [ K D ] = p D B , but the actual K d and K D vary per realization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 1 (Unknown sparsity) . The estimator does not know p d , p D nor the realization-specific K d , K D . It only knows that the support obeys the product structure (3) . The goal is to adaptively infer both the support dimensions and the coefficients from the received data.
texts.text: Remark 1 (Unknown sparsity) . The estimator does not know p d , p D nor the realization-specific K d , K D . It only knows that the support obeys the product structure (3) . The goal is to adaptively infer both the support dimensions and the coefficients from the received data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. AFDM Observation Model
texts.text: C. AFDM Observation Model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now describe how the DD coefficient vector α relates to the received AFDM pilot signals. Let x ∈ C N be the transmitted AFDM pilot. For a DD element at ( l, q ) , the corresponding received AFDM-domain sample at index k is
texts.text: We now describe how the DD coefficient vector α relates to the received AFDM pilot signals. Let x ∈ C N be the transmitted AFDM pilot. For a DD element at ( l, q ) , the corresponding received AFDM-domain sample at index k is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ϕ k,l,q = exp ( j 2 π ( c 1 l 2 -ml N + c 2 ( m 2 -k 2 ) )) x m , (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where m = ( k -q +2 Nc 1 l ) N and ( · ) N denotes the moduloN operation; c 1 , c 2 are AFDM chirp parameters [12].
texts.text: where m = ( k -q +2 Nc 1 l ) N and ( · ) N denotes the moduloN operation; c 1 , c 2 are AFDM chirp parameters [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let P ⊆ { 0 , . . . , N -1 } be the set of pilot observation indices (including guard samples), and let M ≜ |P| . Collecting the received DAFT-domain samples over P yields the observation vector y = [ y k ] k ∈P ∈ C M . For each pair ( l, q ) , define the sensing atom a l,q = [ ϕ k,l,q ] k ∈P ∈ C M . The AFDM sensing matrix is then constructed as M p = [ a 0 , -Q , a 0 , -Q +1 , · · · , a 0 ,Q , a 1 , -Q , · · · , a L -1 ,Q ] ∈ C M × LB .
texts.text: Let P ⊆ { 0 , . . . , N -1 } be the set of pilot observation indices (including guard samples), and let M ≜ |P| . Collecting the received DAFT-domain samples over P yields the observation vector y = [ y k ] k ∈P ∈ C M . For each pair ( l, q ) , define the sensing atom a l,q = [ ϕ k,l,q ] k ∈P ∈ C M . The AFDM sensing matrix is then constructed as M p = [ a 0 , -Q , a 0 , -Q +1 , · · · , a 0 ,Q , a 1 , -Q , · · · , a L -1 ,Q ] ∈ C M × LB .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accordingly, the AFDM pilot observation model can be written compactly as
texts.text: Accordingly, the AFDM pilot observation model can be written compactly as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: y = M p α + w , (5)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where w ∼ CN ( 0 , σ 2 w I M ) . The mapping from a DD pair ( l, q ) to the index in α is given by ι ( l, q ) ≜ lB +( q + Q + 1) , l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , q ∈ {-Q,. . . , Q } . In other words, the coefficient associated with the DD-grid point ( l, q ) is stored at the ι ( l, q ) -th entry of α .
texts.text: where w ∼ CN ( 0 , σ 2 w I M ) . The mapping from a DD pair ( l, q ) to the index in α is given by ι ( l, q ) ≜ lB +( q + Q + 1) , l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , q ∈ {-Q,. . . , Q } . In other words, the coefficient associated with the DD-grid point ( l, q ) is stored at the ι ( l, q ) -th entry of α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overall, the channel estimation problem reduces to: Given y and M p , recover the sparse vector α whose support obeys the Cartesian product structure (3), without knowing the sparsity levels K d and K D in advance.
texts.text: Overall, the channel estimation problem reduces to: Given y and M p , recover the sparse vector α whose support obeys the Cartesian product structure (3), without knowing the sparsity levels K d and K D in advance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: III. A SPARSITY-AGNOSTIC CHANNEL ESTIMATOR
texts.text: III. A SPARSITY-AGNOSTIC CHANNEL ESTIMATOR

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we propose a new channel estimation method that does not require the knowledge of how many delay taps or Doppler bins are active. Instead, it directly estimates the channel from the received pilot signal by exploiting the fact that all active delays share the same Doppler support. The method works in two conceptual steps: (i) it generates candidate supports for different possible sparsity levels, and (ii) it selects the best candidate using BIC. The final channel estimate is obtained by a least-squares fit on the selected support.
texts.text: In this section, we propose a new channel estimation method that does not require the knowledge of how many delay taps or Doppler bins are active. Instead, it directly estimates the channel from the received pilot signal by exploiting the fact that all active delays share the same Doppler support. The method works in two conceptual steps: (i) it generates candidate supports for different possible sparsity levels, and (ii) it selects the best candidate using BIC. The final channel estimate is obtained by a least-squares fit on the selected support.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Problem Reformulation as Structured Model Selection
texts.text: A. Problem Reformulation as Structured Model Selection

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recall from (3) that the true DD support has a Cartesian product structure.
texts.text: Recall from (3) that the true DD support has a Cartesian product structure.

We now define a family of structured supports parameterized by the candidate cardinalities d and r : F ( d, r ) = { S = D × Q : D ⊆ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , |D| = d, Q ⊆ {Q,. . . , Q } , |Q| = r } . The true support belongs to F ( K d , K D ) . The size of this structured family is
texts.text: For any candidate support S ⊆ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } × {-Q,.. . , Q } , we denote by ι ( S ) = { ι ( l, q ) : ( l, q ) ∈ S} the corresponding set of column indices. We now define a family of structured supports parameterized by the candidate cardinalities d and r : F ( d, r ) = { S = D × Q : D ⊆ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , |D| = d, Q ⊆ {Q,. . . , Q } , |Q| = r } . The true support belongs to F ( K d , K D ) . The size of this structured family is

We next develop a structured candidate-generation rule that is consistent with the shared-Doppler geometry and avoids combinatorial search within each ( d, r ) hypothesis.
texts.text: Problem (10) is well-motivated but computationally prohibitive if we were to enumerate all supports in F ( d, r ) exactly. We next develop a structured candidate-generation rule that is consistent with the shared-Doppler geometry and avoids combinatorial search within each ( d, r ) hypothesis.

texts.text: B. Candidate Support Generation

texts.orig: Remark 2. The proposed algorithm is one-stage in the sense that support-dimension selection and channel estimation are integrated into a single structured model-selection procedure. In particular, the method does not first assume ( d, r ) and then apply a separate sparse-recovery routine; instead, it jointly searches over the admissible dimension grid and evaluates each candidate by a common BIC-type criterion.
texts.text: Remark 2. The proposed algorithm is one-stage in the sense that support-dimension selection and channel estimation are integrated into a single structured model-selection procedure. In particular, the method does not first assume ( d, r ) and then apply a separate sparse-recovery routine; instead, it jointly searches over the admissible dimension grid and evaluates each candidate by a common BIC-type criterion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The complete procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1. The complexity is dominated by the ridge inversion (which can be precomputed if the pilot pattern is fixed) and the least-squares fits for each ( d, r ) . Since the grid ( L +1)( B +1) is modest, the algorithm can run efficiently.
texts.text: The complete procedure is summarized in Algorithm 1. The complexity is dominated by the ridge inversion (which can be precomputed if the pilot pattern is fixed) and the least-squares fits for each ( d, r ) . Since the grid ( L +1)( B +1) is modest, the algorithm can run efficiently.

texts.text: The performance of Algorithm 1 is formalized in Proposition 1, wherein we prove the relation between the our channel estimator and the oracle least-squares benchmark (which knows the true support).

texts.text: Proposition 1. If the selected support coincides with the true support, i.e., ̂ S = S ⋆ , the proposed estimator coincides with the oracle least-squares estimator. In particular,

texts.text: Moreover, in the noiseless case, if M S ⋆ has full column rank, then ̂ α = α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. (sketch) If ̂ S = S ⋆ , then (22) is precisely the leastsquares problem posed on the true support. Hence the estimator coincides with the oracle least-squares solution, which proves (23). In the noiseless case, if M S ⋆ has full column rank, then the least-squares solution on the true support is unique and must equal the true coefficient vector, which yields ̂ α = α .
texts.text: Proof. (sketch) If ̂ S = S ⋆ , then (22) is precisely the leastsquares problem posed on the true support. Hence the estimator coincides with the oracle least-squares solution, which proves (23). In the noiseless case, if M S ⋆ has full column rank, then the least-squares solution on the true support is unique and must equal the true coefficient vector, which yields ̂ α = α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our channel estimator addresses the fundamental issue that the true sparsity levels K d and K D are unknown and vary per channel realization. Fixed-budget approaches (e.g., always assuming a constant d and r ) will fail when the actual support dimensions differ. Our method, by contrast, selects the support dimensions directly from the observed data.
texts.text: Our channel estimator addresses the fundamental issue that the true sparsity levels K d and K D are unknown and vary per channel realization. Fixed-budget approaches (e.g., always assuming a constant d and r ) will fail when the actual support dimensions differ. Our method, by contrast, selects the support dimensions directly from the observed data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: IV. SIMULATION RESULTS
texts.text: IV. SIMULATION RESULTS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section evaluates the proposed sparsity-agnostic channel estimator under the AFDM-based DD observation model. The primary goal is to verify whether the proposed method can accurately recover the unknown structured support and achieve near-oracle channel estimation performance without assuming the support cardinalities a priori.
texts.text: This section evaluates the proposed sparsity-agnostic channel estimator under the AFDM-based DD observation model. The primary goal is to verify whether the proposed method can accurately recover the unknown structured support and achieve near-oracle channel estimation performance without assuming the support cardinalities a priori.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Simulation Setup
texts.text: A. Simulation Setup

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider an AFDM frame with N = 4096 , L = 30 , Q = 7 , B = 2 Q + 1 = 15 , and adopt the canonical AFDM pilot configuration with N p = 8 , P afdm = 2 , unless otherwise stated. The DAFT parameters are chosen as c 1 = -P afdm 2 N = -1 4096 , c 2 = 1 20 N . Under this setting, the observation size is |P| = 584 , and the corresponding AFDM pilot overhead equals 685 .
texts.text: We consider an AFDM frame with N = 4096 , L = 30 , Q = 7 , B = 2 Q + 1 = 15 , and adopt the canonical AFDM pilot configuration with N p = 8 , P afdm = 2 , unless otherwise stated. The DAFT parameters are chosen as c 1 = -P afdm 2 N = -1 4096 , c 2 = 1 20 N . Under this setting, the observation size is |P| = 584 , and the corresponding AFDM pilot overhead equals 685 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The channel follows the Bernoulli model in Section II with delay activity probability p d = 0 . 2 and common Doppler activity probability p D = 0 . 2 . Hence, the mean support sizes are s mean d = p d L = 6 , s mean D = p D B = 3 The noise variance follows the full-frame SNR convention σ 2 = N p N · 10 -SNR / 10 , and the ridge regularization parameter is fixed at λ reg = 10 -10 for all SNR points. All curves are averaged over 5000 independent Monte Carlo realizations.
texts.text: The channel follows the Bernoulli model in Section II with delay activity probability p d = 0 . 2 and common Doppler activity probability p D = 0 . 2 . Hence, the mean support sizes are s mean d = p d L = 6 , s mean D = p D B = 3 The noise variance follows the full-frame SNR convention σ 2 = N p N · 10 -SNR / 10 , and the ridge regularization parameter is fixed at λ reg = 10 -10 for all SNR points. All curves are averaged over 5000 independent Monte Carlo realizations.

texts.text: Oracle-LS : least-squares on the true support, serving as a lower performance bound.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. The Normalized Mean-Square Error (NMSE) Performance
texts.text: B. The Normalized Mean-Square Error (NMSE) Performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 shows the average NMSE as a function of SNR for the considered channel estimation schemes. Unlike the fixed shared-count baselines, SBL does not require a prescribed sparsity order and instead promotes sparsity through automatic relevance determination.
texts.text: Figure 1 shows the average NMSE as a function of SNR for the considered channel estimation schemes. Unlike the fixed shared-count baselines, SBL does not require a prescribed sparsity order and instead promotes sparsity through automatic relevance determination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several important observations can be made from Figure 1.
texts.text: Several important observations can be made from Figure 1.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1) First, the proposed method closely follows Oracle-LS over the entire SNR range. This indicates that the proposed structured support search can identify the correct DD support with high probability, so that the final least-squares reconstruction becomes essentially oracle-equivalent. In particular, the proposed method reaches the 10 -4 NMSE regime at moderate SNR, confirming that the unknown support dimensions can be inferred directly from the received observations without sacrificing estimation accuracy.
texts.text: First, the proposed method closely follows Oracle-LS over the entire SNR range. This indicates that the proposed structured support search can identify the correct DD support with high probability, so that the final least-squares reconstruction becomes essentially oracle-equivalent. In particular, the proposed method reaches the 10 -4 NMSE regime at moderate SNR, confirming that the unknown support dimensions can be inferred directly from the received observations without sacrificing estimation accuracy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2) Second, the shared-mean baseline performs poorly across the whole SNR range and exhibits a pronounced error floor. This behavior shows that the dominant error source is not the noise level itself, but the mismatch caused by fixing the support dimensions to their statistical means. Since the true Bernoulli realization fluctuates around these means, a fixed shared-count assumption frequently underestimates the actual support and therefore removes informative coefficients.
texts.text: Second, the shared-mean baseline performs poorly across the whole SNR range and exhibits a pronounced error floor. This behavior shows that the dominant error source is not the noise level itself, but the mismatch caused by fixing the support dimensions to their statistical means. Since the true Bernoulli realization fluctuates around these means, a fixed shared-count assumption frequently underestimates the actual support and therefore removes informative coefficients.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3) Third, the shared-tolerant baseline improves upon the shared-mean baseline, which confirms that enlarging the fixed support budget can partially reduce the risk of support underestimation. However, its performance remains significantly worse than that of the proposed method and still exhibits a clear high-SNR floor. This implies that merely increasing the support budget cannot resolve the underlying model-selection issue. Once the budget is overly enlarged, redundant support entries are retained, which increases the effective fitting dimension and introduces persistent falsealarm errors.
texts.text: Third, the shared-tolerant baseline improves upon the shared-mean baseline, which confirms that enlarging the fixed support budget can partially reduce the risk of support underestimation. However, its performance remains significantly worse than that of the proposed method and still exhibits a clear high-SNR floor. This implies that merely increasing the support budget cannot resolve the underlying model-selection issue. Once the budget is overly enlarged, redundant support entries are retained, which increases the effective fitting dimension and introduces persistent falsealarm errors.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4) Fourth, SBL achieves substantially better NMSE performance than the two fixed-budget baselines, especially at medium and high SNRs. This is expected because SBL does not rely on a manually prescribed support size and can adaptively suppress irrelevant coefficients through its hierarchical sparse prior. Nevertheless, SBL remains consistently worse than the proposed method and Oracle-LS. The performance gap indicates that automatic sparsity promotion alone is insufficient to fully exploit the adopted DS-LTV channel structure. In contrast, the proposed method explicitly searches over the structured DD support family and selects the realization-wise support dimensions through the BIC criterion, leading to more accurate support identification and channel reconstruction.
texts.text: Fourth, SBL achieves substantially better NMSE performance than the two fixed-budget baselines, especially at medium and high SNRs. This is expected because SBL does not rely on a manually prescribed support size and can adaptively suppress irrelevant coefficients through its hierarchical sparse prior. Nevertheless, SBL remains consistently worse than the proposed method and Oracle-LS. The performance gap indicates that automatic sparsity promotion alone is insufficient to fully exploit the adopted DS-LTV channel structure. In contrast, the proposed method explicitly searches over the structured DD support family and selects the realization-wise support dimensions through the BIC criterion, leading to more accurate support identification and channel reconstruction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 supports the central claim of the paper: under realization-wise support fluctuations, neither fixed mean-sized nor enlarged fixed budgets are adequate. SBL offers adaptivity but misses the structural prior, whereas the proposed method achieves near-oracle performance by explicitly leveraging the shared-Doppler geometry and data-driven order selection.
texts.text: Figure 1 supports the central claim of the paper: under realization-wise support fluctuations, neither fixed mean-sized nor enlarged fixed budgets are adequate. SBL offers adaptivity but misses the structural prior, whereas the proposed method achieves near-oracle performance by explicitly leveraging the shared-Doppler geometry and data-driven order selection.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. Exact Support Recovery Probability
texts.text: C. Exact Support Recovery Probability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To further understand the behavior observed in Figure 1, we next examine the exact support recovery probability. Figure 2 presents two heatmaps over the two-dimensional grid formed by the SNR and the number of pilots N p . Figure 2(a) corresponds to the proposed method, while Figure 2(b) corresponds to the shared-mean baseline.
texts.text: To further understand the behavior observed in Figure 1, we next examine the exact support recovery probability. Figure 2 presents two heatmaps over the two-dimensional grid formed by the SNR and the number of pilots N p . Figure 2(a) corresponds to the proposed method, while Figure 2(b) corresponds to the shared-mean baseline.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Figure 2(a), we observe that the proposed method exhibits a clear joint dependence on the pilot budget and the SNR. When the number of pilots is small, the support recovery probability is limited by the reduced number of observations, especially at low SNR. As N p increases, the support recovery probability rises rapidly, and once a moderate pilot budget is available, high support recovery probability is achieved in a broad SNR regime. This demonstrates that the proposed method effectively exploits the shared-Doppler structure and can reliably recover the exact support once the observation budget becomes sufficient.
texts.text: From Figure 2(a), we observe that the proposed method exhibits a clear joint dependence on the pilot budget and the SNR. When the number of pilots is small, the support recovery probability is limited by the reduced number of observations, especially at low SNR. As N p increases, the support recovery probability rises rapidly, and once a moderate pilot budget is available, high support recovery probability is achieved in a broad SNR regime. This demonstrates that the proposed method effectively exploits the shared-Doppler structure and can reliably recover the exact support once the observation budget becomes sufficient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast, Figure 2(b) shows that the shared-mean baseline maintains a very low exact support recovery probability throughout almost the entire ( N p , SNR) plane. Even when both SNR and pilot number increase, the recovery probability remains poor. This observation is particularly important because it indicates that the failure of the fixed shared-mean baseline is not primarily due to noise or observation deficiency. Instead, it is caused by the structural mismatch induced by the fixed support budget. In other words, increasing SNR or adding more pilots cannot fundamentally correct an incorrect model order assumption.
texts.text: In contrast, Figure 2(b) shows that the shared-mean baseline maintains a very low exact support recovery probability throughout almost the entire ( N p , SNR) plane. Even when both SNR and pilot number increase, the recovery probability remains poor. This observation is particularly important because it indicates that the failure of the fixed shared-mean baseline is not primarily due to noise or observation deficiency. Instead, it is caused by the structural mismatch induced by the fixed support budget. In other words, increasing SNR or adding more pilots cannot fundamentally correct an incorrect model order assumption.

texts.text: Delay	index	l

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (d) Shared-tolerant baseline.
texts.text: (d) Shared-tolerant baseline.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: D. Single-Realization Support Visualization
texts.text: D. Single-Realization Support Visualization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 provides a qualitative visualization of the recovered supports for one representative realization at SNR = 20 dB. In this figure, the horizontal axis corresponds to the Doppler bin index q ∈ {-Q,. . . , Q } , the vertical axis corresponds to the delay index l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , and the highlighted entries indicate active coefficients.
texts.text: Figure 3 provides a qualitative visualization of the recovered supports for one representative realization at SNR = 20 dB. In this figure, the horizontal axis corresponds to the Doppler bin index q ∈ {-Q,. . . , Q } , the vertical axis corresponds to the delay index l ∈ { 0 , . . . , L -1 } , and the highlighted entries indicate active coefficients.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The true support in Figure 3(a) clearly exhibits the sharedDoppler structure: the active delay taps share a common Doppler support set. Figure 3(b) shows that the proposed method reconstructs this structure almost exactly. Both the active delay set and the common Doppler support are correctly recovered, which is consistent with the near-oracle NMSE observed in Figure 1.
texts.text: The true support in Figure 3(a) clearly exhibits the sharedDoppler structure: the active delay taps share a common Doppler support set. Figure 3(b) shows that the proposed method reconstructs this structure almost exactly. Both the active delay set and the common Doppler support are correctly recovered, which is consistent with the near-oracle NMSE observed in Figure 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By contrast, Figure 3(c) shows that the shared-mean baseline tends to underestimate the support. Although its assumed support family is consistent with the shared-Doppler geometry, the fixed mean-sized budget is too restrictive for many Bernoulli realizations, which leads to missing active coefficients. On the other hand, Figure 3(d) shows that the shared-tolerant baseline overestimates the support by retaining an enlarged shared support set. This reduces the risk of misses, but introduces additional false alarms and an unnecessarily large fitting dimension.
texts.text: By contrast, Figure 3(c) shows that the shared-mean baseline tends to underestimate the support. Although its assumed support family is consistent with the shared-Doppler geometry, the fixed mean-sized budget is too restrictive for many Bernoulli realizations, which leads to missing active coefficients. On the other hand, Figure 3(d) shows that the shared-tolerant baseline overestimates the support by retaining an enlarged shared support set. This reduces the risk of misses, but introduces additional false alarms and an unnecessarily large fitting dimension.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 visually confirms the interpretation of Figure 1: the shared-mean baseline suffers from underestimation, the shared-tolerant baseline suffers from overestimation, and the proposed method achieves accurate support identification by adaptively selecting the support dimensions from the data.
texts.text: Figure 3 visually confirms the interpretation of Figure 1: the shared-mean baseline suffers from underestimation, the shared-tolerant baseline suffers from overestimation, and the proposed method achieves accurate support identification by adaptively selecting the support dimensions from the data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper developed a sparsity-agnostic DD-domain channel estimator that is practical for real-world systems, where sparsity varies unpredictably across time and propagation environments. The main takeaway is that for sparse channels with regular support, the right question is not 'how sparse?' but 'what is the structure?', and crucially, that structure can be learned from the data.
texts.text: This paper developed a sparsity-agnostic DD-domain channel estimator that is practical for real-world systems, where sparsity varies unpredictably across time and propagation environments. The main takeaway is that for sparse channels with regular support, the right question is not 'how sparse?' but 'what is the structure?', and crucially, that structure can be learned from the data.
